slots.title: The Quiet Architecture of Slow Software
slots.subtitle: There is a second kind of speed — the speed at which a tool gets out of your way.

slots.headline: We measure the machine. We never measure the person.
slots.body: We tune p99 latency to the millisecond and ship it. The p99 of a human's attention — the tax a tool charges just to be used — goes unlogged. Two kinds of speed; we only instrument one.

slots.quote: Slowness is a design budget, not a defect.
slots.attribution: Software designed at the pace of understanding: fewer surfaces, durable defaults, decisions made once and remembered.

slides.layout: 图文混排 (Lead Image + Side Text)

slots.headline: Three forces push teams the other way.
slots.body: None of them is stupidity. Each is a rational response to how software gets funded and judged — which is exactly why they're hard to resist.

stats.label: features in the 'fast' tool nobody can name
features in the 'slow' tool that compose
moves to finish the same job

slots.note: Same job. The slow tool wins not by speed but by subtraction. (Numbers illustrative.)

steps.title: the demo-driven roadmap
steps.body: You build what shows well in a 30-second clip.

steps.title: the metric monoculture
steps.body: Only what is logged is treated as real.

steps.title: the novelty treadmill
steps.body: A redesign is easier to fund than a refinement.

right.label: This
right.text: Subtraction under constraint — and it is fundable if you can price the surface you didn't cut.

slots.headline: Measure time-to-out-of-the-way.
slots.body: Budget for refinement, not just features. Treat the interface a person didn't have to learn as the achievement — and put a number on it.

slots.question: What did your users not have to learn today?
slots.meta: The Quiet Architecture of Slow Software · Mara Vint · Cadence 2026
